topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red 'D-' glared back at me from the graded essay. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I picked up the paper, the cheap printer ink smearing slightly where my finger rested. It wasn't just the mark; it was the crushing realization that all those late nights, the caffeine jitters, the sheer volume of effort I’d poured into that assignment, had amounted to this dismal outcome. I shoved the paper into my backpack with unnecessary force, the sound a dull thud in the otherwise quiet hallway. Walking to my locker felt like wading through thick mud; every step was heavy, my shoulders slumped forward as if gravity had suddenly increased its pull. I avoided eye contact with anyone passing by, focusing instead on the chipped paint on the locker door, wishing I could simply melt into the metal.

[story 2]

"A C-minus? Seriously?" I muttered to myself, the sound barely escaping my throat. My hands were shaking slightly as I stared at the transcript displayed on my phone screen. The numbers seemed to mock me, each one a tiny indictment of my perceived competence. I paced the cramped confines of my dorm room, the worn carpet muffling the sound of my hurried footsteps. I stopped abruptly in front of my desk, running both hands through my hair, pulling at the strands as if trying to physically dislodge the disappointment settling in my chest. The air felt thick, suffocating. I picked up my textbook, but the words swam before my eyes, refusing to coalesce into anything meaningful. I just wanted the weight of the semester to lift, to disappear.

[story 3]

The professor's feedback, starkly written across the margins, felt like a physical slap. I reread the comments three times, the ink blurring slightly under my intense scrutiny. A slow, heavy sigh escaped me, a sound laced with profound weariness. My gaze drifted to the window, where the afternoon sun cast long, accusing shadows across the floor. I leaned my forehead against the cool glass, feeling a distinct pressure behind my eyes. It wasn't anger that surfaced, just a deep, hollow ache. I traced patterns on the condensation, the fleeting moisture a temporary distraction from the sheer weight of falling short of what I had aimed for. I felt utterly drained, as if every reserve of energy had been siphoned away by this single, disappointing result.
